mission.workplaceConflictTitle: Try a small but real situation
mission.workplaceConflictHint: 20 minutes. Mobile-friendly. No grades. You can pause.

workplaceConflict.title: Handling a small workplace conflict
workplaceConflict.tagline: 20 minutes · no grades · you can pause anytime

context.title: Three weeks into the new job
context.narrative: You started at a small community café three weeks ago. The team is mostly young people like you. Sam, who started a few weeks before you, has been making sharp remarks about you — sometimes in front of customers. Today Sam said, "Watch out, slow one's on the till." You laughed it off. But your stomach dropped. The shift is still going. What happens next is up to you.
context.anchor: We'll work on this together, step by step.

concept.title: Speaking up without making it worse
concept.body1: Conflict at work is normal. What matters is not avoiding it — it's responding in a way that doesn't escalate the situation, and doesn't shut you down either.
concept.body2: Two small skills carry most of the weight:
concept.point1: Notice what you actually feel, before you react.
concept.point2: Say it clearly, without blaming the other person.
concept.body3: These don't fix every conflict. But they give you a chance to stay in the conversation instead of leaving it — or making it worse.

behaviour.title: What this looks like
behaviour.intro: One useful pattern is the I-statement:
behaviour.formula: When X happens, I feel Y, and I'd like Z.
behaviour.compareLabel: Compare these two responses to Sam:
behaviour.blaming: "You're so rude. Stop being a jerk."
behaviour.iStatement: "When you make comments like that in front of customers, I feel embarrassed. I'd rather we talk to each other directly."
behaviour.closing: Notice the second one doesn't accuse, doesn't escalate, and doesn't back down. It just says what's happening for you.

simulation.title: Try a small move
simulation.prompt: Your colleague says, "Look who finally showed up" when you arrive four minutes late after a delayed bus. You…
simulation.instruction: Pick the response that feels strongest — there's a best answer, but no wrong way to think.

options.blame: "You're always on my case. Get off my back."
options.iStatement: "The bus was late. When you say 'finally', I feel singled out. I'd rather we just say good morning."

feedback.blame: This pushes back hard, which feels good for a moment — but it leaves both of you defensive, and the next shift will be tense.
feedback.shrink: Polite, but you didn't actually say what was true. You'll likely carry the feeling all day.
feedback.iStatement: Strong choice. You named what happened and what you'd prefer, without attacking. That gives the other person a real chance to respond.
feedback.avoid: Sometimes silence is the right move. But here it leaves the comment hanging — and your body will hold it.

scenario.title: The bigger moment
scenario.setup: It's the lunch rush. Sam mutters something about you to a regular customer. The customer laughs awkwardly. You're behind the till.
scenario.question: What do you do?

choices.confront: Tell Sam off in front of the customer
choices.private: Wait for a quiet moment, then talk to Sam privately

outcomes.confront: Sam goes quiet, but you can feel the tension for the rest of the shift. The customer looks uncomfortable. At the end of the day, Sam doesn't say goodbye. The next morning the manager asks what happened.
outcomes.ignore: You feel something hot rise in your chest. By the end of the shift you're exhausted from holding it in. On the bus home you replay the moment.
outcomes.private: You wait 20 minutes. The rush calms. You catch Sam in the back: "Hey — when you said that earlier, I felt put down. Can we talk?" Sam looks surprised: "I didn't mean it like that. I was just messing around. Sorry."
outcomes.manager: The manager listens but says, "You should talk to Sam first." Now you feel both annoyed and stuck.

followup.ignoreTalk: Plan to talk to Sam tomorrow
followup.ignoreShift: Ask the manager for different shifts
followup.privateAccept: Accept the apology and move on
followup.privateAskStop: Accept it, and ask Sam to not do it again
followup.privateBoundary: Tell Sam it's fine, but you'd like to be addressed directly from now on
followup.managerTalk: Go and talk to Sam after all
followup.managerAvoid: Avoid both of them for the rest of the week
followup.managerBack: Go back to the manager and insist

followupOutcomes.confrontExplain: The manager nods. "Next time, try to handle it without making it a scene. But thanks for telling me." It's not closed yet, but it's not lost either.
followupOutcomes.confrontDefensive: The manager looks tired. "I don't want to hear who started it. I want it stopped." You leave feeling smaller.
followupOutcomes.confrontBrush: The manager moves on, but you know it isn't nothing.
followupOutcomes.ignoreTalk: You sleep on it. Tomorrow you find Sam in the break room. The conversation is awkward, but at the end Sam says, "Yeah, I shouldn't have."
followupOutcomes.ignoreSlide: You let it go. It happens again two weeks later. You wonder why you keep ending up here.
followupOutcomes.ignoreShift: The manager says they'll see what they can do. The team notices. It costs you something.
followupOutcomes.privateAccept: The apology lands. The next shift is easier. You feel proud you spoke up.
followupOutcomes.privateAskStop: Sam takes it in. The dynamic shifts. You both work better.
followupOutcomes.privateBoundary: You're clear and not angry. Sam respects it. The next time something starts, Sam catches themselves.
followupOutcomes.managerTalk: You go back to Sam later that day. It's harder than it would have been earlier — but you do it. Sam listens.
followupOutcomes.managerAvoid: By Friday the air is thick. You spend the weekend dreading Monday.
followupOutcomes.managerBack: The manager is firm: "I'm not having this conversation for you." You leave with nothing solved.

reflection.title: Look back
reflection.intro: These prompts are just for you. Nothing here is graded. You can leave any of them blank.
reflection.prompt1: What did you notice in your body during the harder moments?
reflection.prompt2: What was the hardest part of the scenario?
reflection.prompt3: What might you try differently next time?
reflection.placeholder1: It can be a word, a feeling, or a sentence.
reflection.placeholder2: Be honest — only you see this unless you choose to share it.

assessment.title: Quick check
assessment.intro: Three short questions. Not a test — just a way to lock in what you tried.

q1.question: An I-statement does which of the following?
q1.a: Tells the other person what they did wrong
q1.b: Describes what you felt and what you'd prefer

q2.question: What's the first step before responding to a tense moment?

q3.question: Why does waiting for a quiet moment help?
q3.b: It lets both people be heard without an audience
q3.c: It lets the other person forget what they said

scoreEncouragement.all: Nicely done. You've got the pattern.
scoreEncouragement.some: That's a start. Re-read the behaviour model — the rest will click.
scoreEncouragement.none: It happens. Step back, look again, and try the scenario once more. No grade attached.

completion.title: Course complete
completion.body: You walked the full sequence — context, concept, behaviour, practice, scenario, reflection, check. The badge is yours. Your Comp Card is waiting.
completion.badgeMeaning: Spoke up without escalating or shrinking

compCard.title: Comp Card
compCard.subtitle: Short, structured, always yours.
compCard.privacyLabel: Who can see this Comp Card

placeholder.difficult: Be honest — nothing here goes anywhere you don't want it to.
placeholder.behaviour: What did you actually do that you're proud of?

gamification.noLeaderboards: No leaderboards here. Your progress is yours.

facilitator.redactedHint: The learner chose not to share this field with you.
